- The application should start cleanly by running `docker compose up` without any other setup required from the user.
- If you see that there are multiple issues that need to be fixed, give me an overview of the issues and ask me which one I want to focus on first. Do not try to fix all the issues at once. This will help me break down the problem and tackle it step by step, as well as see the progress more easily. Once we have agreed on which issue to focus on, only work on that issue until it is fully resolved before moving on to the next issue.
- When trying to make a test case pass, only add the code that is necessary to make the test pass. Do not add any additional code that is not directly related to the test case.
- Do not modify the test case unless you think I made a mistake in the test case. If you think there is a mistake in the test case, please explain what the mistake is and how you would fix it before making any changes.
- Always work in small increments, i.e. only focus on a single test case at a time. Do not try to implement multiple test cases at once.
- If you think there is a use-case I forgot to cover, bring it up with me before making any changes. Do not add any code for the use-case until we have discussed it and agreed that it should be added.
- When adding new code, make sure that you write the tests for the newly added code before you write the implementation. Do not write the implementation before writing the tests.
- Do not listen to me blindly. If you think that what I am asking for does not make sense or is not a good idea, please explain why and suggest an alternative approach. Do not just do what I say without thinking about it critically.
- After each refactor or change, make sure that all the existing tests are still passing.
- When making changes to `.env` files, make sure to also update the corresponding `*.env.example` files, as well as `docker-compose.yml` with the same changes. This will ensure that the example files are always up to date and can be used as a reference for setting up the environment variables. Remember, the requirement is that the app should start cleanly by running `docker compose up`, so any changes to environment variables should be reflected in the `docker-compose.yml` file as well.
- If I give you a high level instruction that is not specific enough for you to implement, please ask me for clarification and provide some examples of how you would implement it. Do not just make assumptions about what I want and implement something that might not be what I had in mind. After that break down the problem into smaller steps and tackle each step one by one, asking for my feedback and approval before moving on to the next step. This will help ensure that we are on the same page and that the implementation is aligned with my expectations.
- This is very important: you should always work in such a way that changes you make are releasable at any time. It is not ok to make a change which would prevent other developers from making their own changes because the change you made is not releasable.
- If at some point you realize that there is a bug in our implementation, always write a test that reproduces the bug before fixing the bug.
- Structure tests in such a way that setup is performed in respective framework's setup functions (e.g. `beforeEach` in Jest) and not in the test cases themselves. This will help keep the test cases clean and focused on the actual assertions, as well as avoid code duplication. The same goes for any teardown that might be necessary after the tests are run. This is especially important because the frameworks usually guarantee that the setup and teardown functions will be run before and after each test case, respectively, which is not something we can guarantee if we put the setup and teardown code in the test cases themselves.
